title: Eclipse: Second Dawn for the Galaxy

subtopics.title: Components
subtopics.content:
- **Hex tiles** (space sectors to explore — inner, middle, and outer rings)
- **Player faction boards** (double-layered with slots for influence discs, population cubes, and technology tracks)
- **Influence discs** (used to claim sectors and track economy — the core resource management mechanic)
- **Population cubes** (3 types: Money, Science, Materials — placed on planets)
- **Ship blueprints** (Interceptor, Cruiser, Dreadnought, Starbase — customizable with technology)
- **Ship miniatures** (plastic ships for each player)
- **Technology tiles** (Military, Grid, Nano categories)
- **Discovery tiles** (found when exploring)
- **Reputation tiles** (VP earned from combat)
- **Ambassador tiles** (for diplomacy)
- **Combat dice** (different colors for different weapon types)
- **Damage cubes**
- **Ancient ships, GCDS, and Guardian miniatures** (NPC enemies)
- **Traitor card** and **diplomacy tokens**
- **Round tracker** (9 rounds)

subtopics.title: Board Setup
subtopics.content:
1. Place the **Galactic Center hex** (containing the powerful GCDS guardian) in the center of the table.
2. Shuffle hex tiles into **three stacks**: Inner Ring (I), Middle Ring (II), and Outer Ring (III).
3. Each player places their **home system hex** at the edge, evenly spaced around the center.
4. Place **NPC Ancient ships** on hexes as indicated by exploration tokens.
5. Shuffle and stack **Technology tiles** by category (Military, Grid, Nano) in the tech supply.
6. Place **Reputation tiles** in a face-down supply (4 stacks by tier).
7. Set the **round tracker** to Round 1 of 9.
8. Place discovery tiles, ambassador tiles, and combat dice nearby.

subtopics.title: Player Setup
subtopics.content:
1. Each player chooses a **faction** (Terran or one of several alien species, each with unique abilities and starting configurations).
2. Take your **faction board** — populate it with influence discs covering resource track slots. As you spend discs to claim sectors, you uncover higher resource production.
3. Place starting **population cubes** on your home system's planets (as directed by faction setup).
4. Place your starting **ships** in your home system.
5. Set your **ship blueprints** to their default configurations (each ship type starts with basic components).
6. Take starting **technology tiles** as indicated by your faction.
7. The player with the lowest-numbered faction goes first.

subtopics.title: Game Overview
subtopics.content:
Eclipse: Second Dawn is a 4X space strategy game (eXplore, eXpand, eXploit, eXterminate). Over **9 rounds**, players expand their galactic empires by exploring new sectors, colonizing planets, researching technology, building fleets, engaging in diplomacy, and fighting battles.

The central economic tension is the **influence disc system**: every sector you control and every action you take requires spending influence discs from your faction board. Removing discs uncovers higher resource production — but also commits you to paying upkeep. Overextending your empire will bankrupt you.

At the end of 9 rounds, players score VP from controlled sectors, technologies, reputation tiles (combat victories), discovery bonuses, and other sources. **Highest VP wins**.

subtopics.title: Turn Structure
subtopics.content:
Rounds are divided into **Action Phase** and **Upkeep/Combat Phase**.

## Action Phase
Players take turns in order. On your turn, you perform **one action** (or pass). Once you pass, you cannot take more actions this round. The round ends when all players have passed.

### Available Actions

#### 1. Explore
- Draw a hex tile from the appropriate stack (inner, middle, or outer) and place it adjacent to one of your controlled sectors.
- The hex may contain planets to colonize, discovery tokens, or Ancient NPC ships to fight.
- Place an **influence disc** on the hex to claim it (if you wish and it's accessible via wormhole connections).

#### 2. Influence
- Place influence discs to **claim uncontrolled sectors**, or
- **Remove discs** from sectors to abandon them (freeing up economy).
- You can also rearrange population cubes between your controlled sectors.

#### 3. Research
- Spend **Science resources** to acquire a Technology tile from the supply.
- Place it on your faction board in the corresponding track.
- Technologies provide upgrades for ship blueprints, economic bonuses, or special abilities.

#### 4. Upgrade
- Modify your **ship blueprints** by adding or swapping technology components.
- Each ship type (Interceptor, Cruiser, Dreadnought, Starbase) has a blueprint with slots for weapons, drives, shields, computers, hulls, etc.
- Upgrades apply to ALL ships of that type — both existing and future.

#### 5. Build
- Spend **Material resources** to construct ships or structures.
- Place newly built ships in sectors you control.
- Ship costs vary: Interceptors are cheap, Dreadnoughts are expensive.
- Starbases are immobile defensive structures.

#### 6. Move
- Move your ships between connected sectors (via wormhole links).
- Each ship can move a number of sectors equal to its **drive value**.
- Moving into a sector containing enemy ships or Ancients initiates **combat** (resolved later).

#### 7. Diplomacy (Optional)
- Propose or accept **diplomatic relations** with another player.
- Exchange **Ambassador tiles** — these provide small VP and income bonuses.
- Diplomatic relations mean you cannot attack each other (unless one player **betrays** the agreement, taking the Traitor card as a penalty).

### Passing
- When you pass, you take no more actions this round.
- The earlier you pass, the earlier you act next round (turn order is determined by pass order).
- There is a strategic tension: taking many actions is powerful but costs influence discs and means acting later next round.

## After All Players Pass
1. **Combat Phase**: Resolve all battles in sectors with opposing forces.
2. **Upkeep Phase**: Pay resource costs, produce resources, and clean up.
3. **Advance Round Tracker**.

subtopics.title: Key Actions
subtopics.content:
### Economy — The Influence Disc System
This is the heart of Eclipse's design:
- Your faction board has three **resource tracks**: Money, Science, and Materials.
- Influence discs **cover** track slots. When you spend discs (to claim sectors, take actions), you uncover higher production values.
- **More discs spent = more resources produced per round** BUT also more **upkeep costs** (you must pay money equal to the number of discs deployed).
- If you can't pay upkeep, you must **abandon sectors** (return discs to your board), losing territory.
- Population cubes on planets further boost specific resource production.

### Technology
Technologies are divided into three categories:
- **Military** (red): Weapons (Ion Cannon, Plasma Missile, Antimatter Cannon), targeting computers, shields, hulls.
- **Grid** (yellow): Economic improvements, advanced resource production, wormhole generators.
- **Nano** (green): Biological upgrades, terraforming, orbital facilities.

Researched technologies can be installed on ship blueprints via the Upgrade action. Technology also provides **VP** at game end based on track progress.

### Ship Customization
Each ship blueprint has component slots:
- **Weapons**: Determine attack dice (different weapons roll different dice with different hit probabilities).
- **Drives**: Determine movement speed and initiative.
- **Computers**: Add hit bonuses to attack rolls.
- **Shields**: Subtract from enemy hit rolls.
- **Hulls**: Add hit points.
- **Energy**: Powers components (each component has an energy cost; total must not exceed your reactor output).

Designing efficient ship blueprints is a critical strategic element.

### Combat
When ships from different players (or players vs. Ancients) occupy the same sector:
1. Ships fire in **initiative order** (highest drive/initiative first).
2. Roll attack dice based on weapons installed.
3. Apply **computer bonuses** (add to each die roll) and **shield penalties** (subtract from each enemy die roll).
4. A roll of **6+** (after modifiers) is a hit. Each hit removes 1 hull point.
5. Ships with 0 hull points remaining are destroyed.
6. Continue until one side is eliminated or retreats.
7. The winner draws a **Reputation tile** (VP reward for winning combat).

### Diplomacy and Betrayal
- Diplomatic agreements provide mutual VP and income bonuses.
- **Betraying** a diplomatic agreement lets you attack that player but gives you the **Traitor card** (-2 VP at game end) and the betrayed player gets a reputation bonus.

subtopics.title: Special Rules
subtopics.content:
### Galactic Center
The center hex contains the **Galactic Center Defense System (GCDS)** — an extremely powerful NPC guardian. Defeating it grants the hex (worth 4 VP) and a powerful discovery tile. It requires a strong fleet and good technology.

### Ancient Ships
Many hexes contain Ancient alien ships that must be defeated before the sector can be colonized. They serve as PvE challenges, especially in the early game.

### Discovery Tiles
Explored hexes may contain discovery tokens granting one-time bonuses: resources, VP, unique technologies, or special components for ship blueprints.

### Wormholes
Hexes connect via **wormhole symbols** on their edges. Ships can only move between sectors connected by matching wormholes. Some technologies create additional wormhole connections.

### Population and Planets
Each hex may contain planets of different types (Money/orange, Science/pink, Materials/brown, Wild/gray). Colonize planets by placing population cubes to boost resource production. Advanced planets (gray) require specific technology to colonize.

### Faction Asymmetry
Each alien faction has unique starting setups, special abilities, and sometimes unique technologies or ship blueprints. The Terran faction is the most balanced/beginner-friendly.

### Turn Order
Turn order for the next round is determined by **pass order** in the current round. Passing first gives you first-player advantage next round. This creates a tension: do more now (but act later next round) or pass early (fewer actions but better position next round).

subtopics.title: Scoring and Endgame
subtopics.content:
The game ends after **Round 9**. Final scoring:

| Category | Details |
|---|---|
| **Controlled Sectors** | Each hex you control is worth its printed VP (1-4 VP depending on location) |
| **Reputation Tiles** | VP from combat victories (drawn after winning battles, values range from 1-4) |
| **Ambassador Tiles** | 1 VP each from diplomatic agreements still in effect |
| **Technology Tracks** | VP for progress on each technology track |
| **Discovery Tiles** | Some discoveries provide end-game VP |
| **Traitor Card** | -2 VP if you hold the Traitor card |
| **Species Bonuses** | Some factions have unique scoring conditions |

The player with the **most VP** wins. Ties are broken by resources remaining.

subtopics.title: Beginner Tips
subtopics.content:
- **Don't overexpand**. Every sector you claim costs an influence disc and increases upkeep. Expand only as fast as your economy can support.
- **Economy first**. Build a solid economic foundation in rounds 1-3 before investing heavily in military.
- **Upgrade before building**. Design your ship blueprints with researched tech BEFORE building a fleet. Upgrading blueprints retroactively applies to existing ships.
- **Watch upkeep costs**. Going bankrupt forces you to abandon sectors — a devastating setback.
- **Explore aggressively early**. Good hexes go fast. Explore in rounds 1-2 to secure valuable territory.

subtopics.title: Key Strategies
subtopics.content:
- **Economic engine**: Prioritize uncovering high-value resource track slots early. A strong economy lets you take more actions per round.
- **Tech rushing**: Investing heavily in Science and technology early can give you overwhelmingly superior ships by mid-game.
- **Military pressure**: Even if you don't attack, having a strong fleet deters opponents from attacking you and forces them to invest in defense.
- **Blueprint optimization**: The most efficient ship designs maximize damage output per resource spent. Plasma Missiles + Computers is a classic combo. Don't waste slots on unnecessary components.
- **Pass timing**: Passing early to secure first-player next round is powerful — especially in rounds 7-9 when positioning for final conflicts matters.
- **Diplomacy as economy**: Ambassador tiles provide VP and income. Diplomatic relationships reduce the need for defensive spending.
- **Galactic Center timing**: Taking the center hex is worth 4 VP and a discovery, but requires significant military investment. Time your assault carefully.
- **Deny opponents**: Controlling chokepoint hexes can block opponents from expanding. Hexes with wormhole connections to multiple sectors are strategically valuable.
